C aregiver A ssessment, Evidence Summary, Financial C larification, Opportunity Loss, C aregiver Fatigue, Emergency Risk, and Role Transition Prep ared by: Cody D o cumen t typ e: Caregiver assessment / boundary record / evidence packet Status: Working master copy A documented statement of role limits, observed patterns, evidence-based care concerns, compensation mismatch, financial clarification, emergency-only risk, and transition plan. Prepared by: Cody Date: May 24, 2026 Recipient: Lisa / care-related parties as needed This do cumen t is n o t written to shame, p un ish, diagn o se, o r attack. It is written because I care en o ugh to do cumen t what is hap p en in g clearly, p ro fessio n ally, an d with eviden ce. I co uld min imize this, ign o re it, o r walk away. In stead, I am p uttin g the facts, care co n cern s, an d bo un daries in writin g o n e fin al time. Purpose and Ground Rules This document is being created to clearly state what has happened, what I have observed, what outside evidence supports my concerns, what I can no longer continue doing, and what needs to happen moving forward. Each section stands on its own. These issues should not be blended together or used to cancel each other out. Lane A — Employment and LaborUnpaid or underpaid caregiving work, hours, and scope mismatch. 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 1/19

# Page 2^page=2
Lane B — Direct Financial AccountingMoney sent, money received, bills, shared expenses, and net balance. Lane C — Opportunity LossIncome disruption, missed earning windows, botched trips, delayed rental access, and lost work capacity. Lane D — Historical Support + Care BoundariesLong-term support history, observed patterns, care impact, emergency risk, and what I will or will not do moving forward. I am do cumen tin g facts an d o bservatio n s. I am n o t diagn o sin g. A n ythin g clin ical sho uld be reviewed with do cto rs, p harmacists, n urses, case man agers, men tal-health p ro fessio n als, o r o ther q ualified p ro viders. Why This Is an Intervention, Not an Attack I am saying this because I love you and because I do not want to watch fear, denial, confusion, or reactive patterns keep hurting you while everyone acts like nothing else needs to be addressed. This is not about calling you “crazy.” It is not about blaming you for having COPD, trauma, anxiety, fear, or pain. It is about recognizing that physical illness and psychological distress can interact. That interaction is real, common, and medically recognized. In terven tio n statemen t:I am n o t sayin g this to shame yo u. I am sayin g it because as lo n g as p sycho lo gical distress is den ied, min imized, o r treated as “just my lun gs,” the full p ro blem can n o t be treated. If yo u un derstan d the facts an d still cho o se n o t to address that p art, that is yo ur decisio n . B ut I can n o t silen tly p articip ate in p reten din g it is n o t p art o f the care p icture. You have told me things that point to trauma, PTSD-type symptoms, nightmares, fear, and psychological distress. Then at other times you tell me nothing is psychologically wrong and that it is only your lungs. Both cannot be the full truth at the same time. The issue is not whether your lungs are real. They are. The issue is whether fear, panic, trauma, anxiety, and control loops are making your breathing episodes worse and making care harder. This do cumen t is eviden ce that I care. I am tryin g to help yo u face the full care p icture in stead o f lettin g the same cycle co n tin ue blin dly. COPD, Anxiety , Panic, and the Breathing-Fear Loop — Evidence Summary COPD is a real lung disease that makes breathing difficult because airway/lung damage blocks airflow. Nothing in this document denies that. 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 2/19

# Page 3^page=3
At the same time, COPD is strongly connected with emotional distress. The American Lung Association states that sadness, fear, and worry are common when living with COPD, and that persistent emotional symptoms affecting daily life may represent anxiety or depression. The American Lung Association also describes a vicious cycle: trouble breathing can increase anxiety and depression, and anxiety/depression can worsen quality of life, make breathing feel harder, and increase the likelihood of hospitalization with COPD flareups. Eviden ce p o in t:Research do es n o t sup p o rt sep aratin g C OPD fro m an xiety as if they can n o t in teract. A n xiety an d dep ressio n are reco gn ized as imp o rtan t an d o ften un derdiagn o sed co mo rbidities in C OPD . Panic attacks and panic disorder are also relevant. A review found that panic disorder in COPD may be up to 10 times more common than in the general population. Other research reports wide prevalence ranges for depression and anxiety in COPD populations, with depression estimates from 8–80% and anxiety estimates from 2–96%, depending on the study and measurement approach. Imp o rtan t clarificatio n :I am n o t sayin g “it is o n ly an xiety.” I am sayin g an xiety, p an ic, trauma, fear, an d C OPD can feed each o ther. Treatin g o n ly the lun gs while den yin g the fear lo o p can leave the cycle in tact. What I Am Asking You to Face I am asking you to consider that the following may all be true at once: Your COPD and breathing symptoms are real. Your pain is real. Your fear is real. Your trauma history may be affecting how your body and mind respond during episodes. Anxiety or panic can make breathing feel worse, even when oxygen numbers or other objective signs are reassuring. Refusing to discuss anxiety, panic, trauma, PTSD symptoms, or psychological support may keep the loop going. This is the p art I n eed yo u to hear:n eedin g men tal-health sup p o rt do es n o t mean yo u are weak, fake, dramatic, o r “crazy.” It mean s yo ur n ervo us system is un der stress an d yo ur care p lan n eeds to in clude that reality. When you say one minute that you have trauma, PTSD, nightmares, and fear, but the next minute say there is nothing psychological involved, that makes it impossible to build a stable plan. The care team needs the whole picture. I am asking you to discuss anxiety, panic, trauma, sleep/nightmares, and PTSD symptoms with your doctor or a mental-health professional. I am not diagnosing you. I am identifying patterns that need professional review. 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 3/19

# Page 4^page=4
My bo un dary is n o t “han dle it p erfectly.” My bo un dary is: sto p den yin g the p art o f the p ro blem that may be treatable. Employment and Labor — Separate Issue This section is only about caregiving labor, time, scope, and compensation. It is not about whether I personally owe money or whether you owe me money in the family/accounting sense. The caregiving role became much larger than the paid structure supported. I was not simply doing small errands. I was functioning across multiple roles: caregiver, transporter, coordinator, scheduler, cleaner, medication organizer, ER/discharge transition support, emotional support, household stabilizer, and default responder. C atego ry A p p ro ximate A mo un t / D escrip tio n Reported/expected paid rate $14/hour Paid hours often received Approximately 16–20 hours/week due to clock-in/clock-out and scheduling issues Approximate weekly paid amount $224–$280/week Actual workload estimate Approximately 70–80 hours/week when including active care, call-ins, errands, preparation, coordination, overnight disruption, and reactive needs Estimated labor value at $14/hour 70 hrs = $980/week; 80 hrs = $1,120/week Estimated uncompensated weekly labor Approx. $700–$896/week depending on paid hours and actual workload Emp lo ymen t/labo r co n clusio n : the care wo rklo ad exp an ded far beyo n d the p aid ro le. This created a serio us co mp en satio n an d ro le-sco p e mismatch. This labor section does not include family help, prior financial support, direct money exchanged, use of the Jeep, housing support, or other separate financial issues. 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 4/19

# Page 5^page=5
Caregiver Burden Evidence — Why This Load Is Not Sustainable Caregiver burnout is medically recognized. Cleveland Clinic defines caregiver burnout as physical, emotional, and mental exhaustion that can happen while taking care of someone else, and notes that stressed caregivers may experience fatigue, anxiety, and depression. Research describes caregiving as a chronic stress experience that creates physical and psychological strain over extended periods of time. CDC data show caregivers have higher prevalence of frequent mental distress and lifetime diagnosed depression compared with non-caregivers. National caregiving data show that U.S. caregivers provide an average of 27 hours of care per week, and 24% provide 40 or more hours weekly. Half of working caregivers experience employment impacts. C o n text:My estimated 70–80 ho urs/week is far beyo n d the n atio n al average caregiver lo ad an d abo ve even the 40+ ho ur high-in ten sity caregivin g catego ry rep o rted in n atio n al data. This is why I am not being dramatic when I say the arrangement is harming my health, income, stability, and recovery. The burden level itself is a known risk. C o n tin uin g to rely o n o n e exhausted p erso n as the care system is un safe fo r bo th o f us. Comfort Keepers' Own Materials Support Respite and Role Boundaries This section is included because even caregiving organizations describe family caregiving as demanding work that can affect a caregiver's physical, emotional, and mental well-being. That matters because this is not simply me being dramatic, lazy, selfish, or unwilling. Comfort Keepers' respite-care materials describe family caregivers as handling household tasks, self-care assistance, supervision, mobility support, emotional support, and social support. They state that these demands can take a toll on caregivers' physical, emotional, and mental well-being, and that respite care gives caregivers time to rest, recharge, and attend to their own needs. Comfort Keepers' own language also describes respite care as a way to prevent caregiver burnout and says that respite allows caregivers to step away temporarily so they can provide better care in the long run. Extern al sup p o rt p o in t:The same typ e o f agen cy in vo lved in this care situatio n reco gn izes that caregivers n eed breaks, ro le clarity, p lan n in g, an d backup sup p o rt. A caregiver bein g o verwhelmed, exhausted, resen tful, iso lated, o r un able to balan ce caregivin g with life is n o t a character flaw. It is a reco gn ized sign that resp ite an d structured sup p o rt are n eeded. Signs described in Comfort Keepers' caregiver materials Feeling overwhelmed or exhausted frequently or almost constantly Physical or mental health being significantly affected by caregiving 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 5/19

# Page 6^page=6
Difficulty balancing caregiving with personal, social, or work life Increased irritability, sleep disturbances, or feelings of resentment Guilt or hesitation about seeking respite, even when support is necessary If a p ro fessio n al caregivin g co mp an y says resp ite is n ecessary an d that caregiver exhaustio n can harm bo th the caregiver an d the care relatio n ship , then it is n o t reaso n able to exp ect me to keep fun ctio n in g as the un p aid, o n -call, emo tio n ally o verlo aded safety n et. This supports the boundary that outside caregiving support is not optional. It is part of making the home-care arrangement safer and more sustainable. Preventive Care Is Real Care — I Am Not a Personal Servant One repeated issue has been the statement: “I did not ask you to do that.” That statement ignores how caregiving actually works. Caregiving is not limited to whatever request is spoken out loud in the moment. Real caregiving includes noticing risks, preparing the environment, preventing predictable problems, organizing supplies, tracking changes, reducing hazards, and creating systems before something becomes an emergency. I have been using education, empathy, pattern recognition, systems thinking, and crisis-management skills to prevent problems before they turn into bigger problems. That is not me “doing extra” for no reason. That is me trying to protect the care situation from becoming more chaotic, dangerous, expensive, or legally risky. I am n o t a highly educated, emp athetic chao s-whisp erer so I can be treated like a servan t. If my judgmen t, p lan n in g, an d p reven tio n wo rk are n o t resp ected, then I can n o t also be held resp o n sible when the p reven table p ro blems hap p en . Examples of preventive care work Sanitizing and organizing the care area before it becomes unsafe Replacing pads, cleaning surfaces, and staging supplies before hygiene problems escalate Tracking medications, bottles, pharmacy issues, and discharge changes so confusion does not become a medication error Putting supplies within reach so transfers, pain, breathing episodes, and panic do not escalate unnecessarily Noticing when communication patterns are increasing anxiety or making symptoms worse Trying to ask care-team questions before a vague instruction becomes a fixed rule or future conflict Trying to preserve income and transportation so care needs do not financially collapse the whole household If I am to ld my p reven tive wo rk do es n o t co un t because it was n o t sp ecifically req uested, then my resp o n sibility must also be limited. I can n o t be den ied autho rity an d then assign ed blame. 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 6/19

# Page 7^page=7
The correct structure is not “Cody does everything silently until something breaks.” The correct structure is a written care plan, scheduled support, respite coverage, emergency escalation instructions, and clear division of responsibilities. Blurred Employment Lines, Hours, and Overtime Risk This section is not legal advice. It is a documentation concern that needs to be reviewed by the employer, caregiver agency, case manager, or other appropriate party. The caregiving arrangement has become so blurred that it is unclear where paid work ends, family help begins, on-call responsibility starts, and unpaid labor is being assumed. That creates a serious risk that hours, overtime, wage rules, and role expectations are not being handled correctly. Concerns needing formal review Late clock-ins or late clock-outs reducing paid hours below the actual time worked Paid hours showing approximately 16–20 hours/week while actual care burden was far higher Care tasks continuing outside paid windows because the need did not stop when the clock stopped Unclear distinction between employment duties and family obligations On-call or emergency expectations being implied without proper structure or compensation Possible overtime or hour-limit concerns if the actual work performed exceeds allowed or documented hours When the lin es are this blurred, everyo n e is exp o sed: the caregiver, the care recip ien t, the agen cy/emp lo yer, an d the care p lan . The an swer is n o t mo re in fo rmal relian ce o n me. The an swer is written ro le clarity an d fo rmal schedulin g. Emp lo ymen t/co mp lian ce co n clusio n : I n eed the ro le, ho urs, p ay, o n -call exp ectatio n s, an d bo un daries clarified in writin g. Un til then , I can n o t co n tin ue fun ctio n in g as un p aid o verflo w labo r. Emergency-Only Backup Is Not a Safe Role for Me You stated that your plan is to manage your own medications, organization, and daily care, and that you mainly need me for emergencies or hospital trips. That plan does not reduce my burnout. It removes the parts of care where I am strongest — organizing, documenting, preparing, coordinating, preventing problems, and seeing risks ahead of time — and leaves me with the part that is most psychologically destabilizing, least controllable, and most legally risky: emergency response after something has already gone wrong. 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 7/19

# Page 8^page=8
Emergen cy reality:If there is a true emergen cy, the ap p ro p riate resp o n se is to call 911 o r the p ro p er medical/emergen cy sup p o rt system. I can be n o tified as yo ur so n o r emergen cy co n tact, but I can n o t be the emergen cy p lan . Why this matters An emergency-only role gives me responsibility without authority. It expects me to respond after escalation rather than allowing me to prevent escalation. It creates legal and emotional risk if something goes wrong while I am unavailable, asleep, working, driving, or recovering. It places the highest-stress responsibility on the person least able to safely carry that specific role right now. It is not a care plan. It is a gap in the care plan. I am willin g to help within my limits. I am n o t willin g to be treated as the substitute fo r 911, ho me health, case man agemen t, scheduled caregivin g, medicatio n reco n ciliatio n , o r a written discharge p lan . If you want to manage your own daily organization and medication setup, that needs to be reviewed with your care team and documented. If the plan depends on me only when things become urgent, then the plan is unsafe and incomplete. That matters because this is not simply me being dramatic, lazy, selfish, or unwilling. They state that these demands can take a toll on caregivers' physical, emotional, and mental well-being, and that respite care gives caregivers time to rest, recharge, and attend to their own needs. Comfort Keepers' own language also describes respite care as a way to prevent caregiver burnout and says that respite allows caregivers to step away temporarily so they can provide better care in the long run. It is a reco gn ized sign that resp ite an d structured sup p o rt are n eeded. Preparing the home/care area after ER or EMS disruption Cleaning, sanitizing, replacing pads, organizing supplies, and resetting the care space Medication organization and attempting to track changes after ER/hospital visits Transporting or delivering medical equipment, including scooter and oxygen tank Retrieving multiple bags of medical items and supplies Handling errands, pharmacy needs, equipment logistics, and household needs 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 10/19

# Page 11^page=11
Fielding repeated calls/texts during work, rest, or recovery time Trying to coordinate with Humana/case management, Comfort Keepers, home health, PT, nurses, and providers Monitoring symptoms, vitals, anxiety/breathing episodes, pain reports, mobility/transfer risk, and home safety issues Managing emotional escalation when care instructions are unclear or fear takes over Why “I did not ask you to do that” does not solve the issue A caregiver is not just a personal errand runner. A caregiver is expected to notice risks, prevent problems, prepare the environment, support the care plan, and respond to predictable needs. Many of the things I did were done because I could see problems coming and tried to prevent them before they became bigger emergencies. The fact that so methin g was n o t verbally req uested do es n o t mean it was un n ecessary o r o utside the real care burden . Direct Financial Accounting — Separate Cash-Based Issue This section is separate from unpaid labor. It is only about direct financial exchange: money sent, money received, bills, shared expenses, and immediate household/care-related balances. Based on my current tracking and recollection, after accounting for money I have sent, money you have sent, expenses covered, and shared obligations, we appear to be approximately balanced, or close to net zero. If anything, there may be a possible over-contribution on my side, but I am not trying to nickel-and-dime every small item. Fin an cial C atego ry Status / C larificatio n Money I have sent Includes support for bills, insurance, household/care needs, and other direct assistance Money received from you Includes money sent back or given toward household/care-related needs Shared expenses Includes food, supplies, transportation, equipment movement, bills, and related needs Current conclusion Approximately net zero / financially balanced, based on available tracking D irect fin an cial co n clusio n : I am n o t o p eratin g fro m a p o sitio n where I o we yo u o n go in g mo n ey. The rep eated imp licatio n that I am takin g fro m yo u o r o we yo u is n o t sup p o rted by the bro ader fin an cial reco rd. 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 11/19

# Page 12^page=12
This section does not include unpaid labor value, opportunity loss, lost income, delayed rental access, missed work days, botched travel, or historical support such as long-term vehicle use or prior weekly support. Opportunity Loss and Income Disruption This section is separate from employment/labor compensation, direct financial accounting, and historical support. It documents income opportunities lost or disrupted because the care situation repeatedly expanded, delayed, destabilized, or interfered with my ability to work. This is not included to demand repayment in this document. It is included because the repeated narrative that I have not contributed, have taken too much, or owe money ignores the economic damage caused by the instability of this caregiving arrangement. Known opportunity-loss factors The botched Indianapolis trip disrupted income, timing, planning, and stability. In March, I overpaid / fronted too much money relative to what was actually stable or available afterward. When I wanted to use a rental vehicle to generate income while you were sleeping, my money was effectively held up and the rental/income plan was delayed or blocked. The expectation became that I would receive approximately $100/day, but delays, missing pay, missed days, and expanding workload made that unreliable. Each delay created a ripple effect: less income, more stress, more scrambling, more dependence on short-term fixes, and less ability to stabilize myself. As the care situation intensified, my workload doubled, then tripled, then quadrupled into a role that no longer matched the pay, agreement, or capacity I had available. C atego ry Estimated Eco n o mic Imp act No tes Expected caregiving income Approx. $100/day when paid as expected Unreliable when pay was delayed, missed, or reduced by hours/clock issues Lost work days $100/day minimum baseline Each missed day affected income and ability to stabilize Rental / Lyft income opportunity Variable; needs ledger estimate Rental access was intended to generate income during available hours, including when you were sleeping or hospitalized Botched Indy trip Variable; needs itemized trip estimate Includes gas, time, missed work, delay, stress, and lost earning window Expanded workload Indirect economic loss More caregiving hours meant fewer income-producing hours and less recovery time 6/14/26, 9:09 PM Caregiver Assessment Packet v6 file:///C:/Users/codyr/Downloads/preview.html 12/19

For clean accounting, this section should eventually be backed by a separate ledger showing dates, expected income, actual income, missed work days, blocked/delayed transportation access, trip costs, and care-related interruptions. Historical Support — Separate Long-T erm Contribution Pattern This section documents long-term support history. This is separate from current direct accounting and separate from the employment/labor issue. Regular financial support in the past, including periods of approximately $200/week Providing use of a vehicle for an extended period without requiring payment for that use Contributing toward housing or place-related costs Sending money for bills and insurance Helping with expenses because we are family, not because every item was itemized I am not using this section to demand repayment. I am documenting it because it matters when money is repeatedly brought up as if I have only taken or failed to contribute. # Page 14^page=14
When I wanted to use a rental vehicle to generate income while you were sleeping, my money was effectively held up and the rental/income plan was delayed or blocked. The expectation became that I would receive approximately $100/day, but delays, missing pay, missed days, and expanding workload made that unreliable. Each delay created a ripple effect: less income, more stress, more scrambling, more dependence on short-term fixes, and less ability to stabilize myself. As the care situation intensified, my workload doubled, then tripled, then quadrupled into a role that no longer matched the pay, agreement, or capacity I had available. Care Coordination Concerns The biggest care problem is not that no one cares. The problem is that the care is fragmented, reactive, and underdocumented. Too many things are happening verbally, emotionally, or in crisis mode instead of through a clear written plan. Medication changes and hospital/ER instructions are not always clearly reconciled for home use. Discharge and return-home transitions need coordination before you are sent home, especially if I am working, resting, driving, or unavailable.
